stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar called to the emergency department to assess a 42-year-old non-Aboriginal man brought in with confusion and rigidity. He was started on haloperidol 10 mg three days ago for acute psychosis. He had received no other psychotropic medication. On arrival he is sweating, mute, and stiff with cogwheeling in all four limbs. Family deny illicit drug use and there has been no recent infection.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 °C, HR 132, BP 168/96, RR 28, SpO2 95% room air, GCS 12
data_table.Examination: Generalised lead-pipe rigidity, tremor, diaphoresis, no clonus, no neck stiffness, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Creatine kinase 6200 U/L, WCC 13.5 x10^9/L, urea 9.2, creatinine 142 micromol/L, K 4.4, Na 142, urine myoglobin positive, CT brain normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue haloperidol at reduced dose with paracetamol and active cooling because pyrexia is from the acute psychosis rather than from the medication
options.rationale: Continuing the offending dopamine antagonist worsens neuroleptic malignant syndrome and risks death; immediate cessation is required. (Source: RANZCP Psychopharmacology Resources)

options.text: Add lorazepam 2 mg orally and observe for response with no other change in psychotropic medication because rigidity is characteristic of catatonia not NMS
options.rationale: Hyperthermia, autonomic instability and CK over 1000 with recent dopamine antagonist exposure point to NMS rather than isolated catatonia; haloperidol must be ceased and supportive care escalated. (Source: RANZCP Psychopharmacology Resources)

options.text: Cease haloperidol immediately, transfer to intensive care for active cooling, intravenous hydration, dantrolene or bromocriptine and electrolyte monitoring with myoglobinuric renal protection
options.rationale: NMS requires immediate cessation of the offending agent, aggressive supportive care including cooling and fluid resuscitation, and consideration of dantrolene or bromocriptine; renal protection addresses myoglobinuric injury. (Source: RANZCP Psychopharmacology Resources)

options.text: Switch haloperidol to olanzapine 10 mg, continue current observation in the medical ward and reassess in 24 hours after a single trial of cyproheptadine 12 mg orally
options.rationale: Switching to another antipsychotic continues dopamine blockade and worsens NMS; cyproheptadine treats serotonin syndrome, which has clonus and hyperreflexia rather than lead-pipe rigidity. (Source: RANZCP Psychopharmacology Resources)

options.text: Administer intramuscular procyclidine 10 mg as the first-line antidote and continue haloperidol in expectation that cogwheel rigidity will resolve within an hour
options.rationale: Procyclidine treats acute dystonia and parkinsonian extrapyramidal side effects, not NMS; continuing haloperidol perpetuates the syndrome. (Source: RANZCP Psychopharmacology Resources)

explanation.summary: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is a life-threatening idiosyncratic reaction to dopamine antagonists. Diagnosis rests on recent exposure plus the tetrad of hyperthermia, generalised rigidity, autonomic instability and altered mental state, with creatine kinase typically above 1000 U/L. Management is immediate cessation of the offending agent, supportive care (cooling, intravenous fluids, electrolyte correction, myoglobinuric renal protection), intensive care admission and pharmacological options of dantrolene, bromocriptine or amantadine. Distinguish from serotonin syndrome (clonus, hyperreflexia, recent serotonergic exposure) and malignant catatonia.
key_points: Immediate cessation of the offending dopamine antagonist is the first priority
Supportive care: cooling, intravenous fluids, electrolyte correction
Renal protection for rhabdomyolysis with myoglobinuria
Dantrolene or bromocriptine for severe cases in intensive care
Differentiate from serotonin syndrome (clonus, hyperreflexia) and catatonia
explanation.pearls: Rechallenge with a different antipsychotic is possible after at least 2 weeks of full recovery, ideally with a low-potency atypical agent and slow titration.

subtopic_detail: Borderline personality disorder with recurrent self-harm
stem: You are the emergency department registrar reviewing a 26-year-old non-Aboriginal woman with known borderline personality disorder who presents after superficial wrist lacerations following an argument with her partner. She is engaged in weekly dialectical behaviour therapy. She has had four similar presentations this year. She is now calm, engaged, declines admission and has a written safety plan in her phone. Her partner is present and supportive.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 78, BP 118/72, RR 16, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Superficial linear lacerations to left forearm, no neurovascular compromise, no tendon injury; mood reactive, no current suicidal intent reported
data_table.Investigations: Bedside urine drug screen negative, BSL 5.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disposition?

options.text: Admit involuntarily to the psychiatric unit under the relevant Mental Health Act because the repeated self-harm episodes constitute risk of serious harm
options.rationale: Hospital admission for chronic recurrent self-harm in borderline personality disorder is generally not therapeutic and can be iatrogenic; outpatient psychological therapy is the evidence-based core treatment. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Prescribe diazepam 5 mg three times daily for two weeks and arrange general practitioner review without communication with her existing DBT therapist about the presentation
options.rationale: Routine benzodiazepines have no role in chronic borderline personality disorder and risk dependence and disinhibition; communication with the existing treating team is essential. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Wound care, review and update of the existing safety plan, communication with her DBT therapist, and discharge with planned follow-up within 24 to 48 hours
options.rationale: Best-practice care for borderline personality disorder presenting after non-lethal self-harm involves wound management, brief safety planning, communication with the treating team and timely outpatient follow-up rather than admission. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Initiate quetiapine 100 mg at night to reduce emotional dysregulation and arrange a private psychiatrist review within 6 weeks without contacting her current treating team
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy has no first-line role for the core features of borderline personality disorder, and bypassing the existing treating team fragments care. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for an inpatient electroconvulsive therapy course because of the chronic and recurrent nature of the self-harm and limited response to weekly psychotherapy
options.rationale: ECT has no role in the treatment of borderline personality disorder; it is reserved for severe treatment-resistant depression or catatonia. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Australian NHMRC guidelines establish structured psychological therapies, in particular dialectical behaviour therapy and mentalisation-based therapy, as the core treatment for borderline personality disorder. Hospital admission is generally avoided for chronic recurrent self-harm because it can reinforce maladaptive patterns and rarely changes long-term trajectory. Acute presentations focus on physical wound care, brief safety planning, communication with the existing treating team and timely outpatient follow-up. Pharmacotherapy has at most an adjunct role for comorbid conditions and short-term crisis use.
key_points: Structured psychological therapy is the core treatment for borderline personality disorder
Routine hospital admission for recurrent self-harm is not therapeutic
Acute care focus is wound management, brief safety planning, team communication, follow-up
Medications have at most adjunct roles for comorbid conditions
Benzodiazepines should generally be avoided because of dependence and disinhibition risk
explanation.pearls: Brief admission may be considered when there is a clear acute risk above baseline or comorbid major depression, but should be planned, time-limited and discharge focused.

stem: You are the emergency registrar in a metropolitan hospital with onsite primary percutaneous coronary intervention service. A 58-year-old non-Aboriginal man presents 50 minutes after the onset of central crushing chest pain radiating to his left arm. He has been given aspirin 300 mg orally by the paramedics. He has no contraindications to dual antiplatelet therapy and no bleeding diathesis. His pain is now 7 out of 10 and he is diaphoretic.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 96, BP 138/82, RR 20, SpO2 96% room air
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, chest clear, heart sounds dual without murmur, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: ECG: 2 mm ST elevation V2 to V5 with reciprocal inferior depression, troponin pending, FBE and U&E normal, INR 1.0

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence tenecteplase weight-adjusted intravenous bolus and admit to the coronary care unit with rescue percutaneous coronary intervention if no resolution at 90 minutes
options.rationale: Fibrinolysis is reserved for STEMI presenting where primary PCI is not available within 90 minutes from first medical contact; here PCI is onsite and is preferred. (Source: CSANZ STEMI Guidelines)

options.text: Administer aspirin 300 mg, an additional oral P2Y12 inhibitor and arrange transfer for elective angiography within 24 hours during normal working hours
options.rationale: Delaying revascularisation in a STEMI by 24 hours loses myocardium; reperfusion must be immediate, preferably by primary PCI within 90 minutes of first medical contact. (Source: CSANZ STEMI Guidelines)

options.text: Loading dose ticagrelor 180 mg orally, intravenous heparin and immediate activation of the cardiac catheter laboratory for primary PCI within 90 minutes of first contact
options.rationale: Anterior STEMI within 12 hours of onset at a PCI-capable hospital requires immediate primary PCI within 90 minutes of first medical contact with dual antiplatelet loading and anticoagulation. (Source: CSANZ STEMI Guidelines)

options.text: Order CT coronary angiogram to confirm the diagnosis before deciding on revascularisation strategy, given that troponin has not yet been reported back as elevated
options.rationale: STEMI is an ECG diagnosis and immediate reperfusion is not delayed for troponin or CT confirmation; CT coronary angiography has no role in acute STEMI. (Source: CSANZ STEMI Guidelines)

options.text: Manage medically with intravenous beta-blocker, statin and angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor in the coronary care unit and reassess for invasive strategy in 72 hours
options.rationale: Conservative management without reperfusion in STEMI within the window of opportunity results in avoidable myocardial loss and worse outcomes. (Source: CSANZ STEMI Guidelines)

explanation.summary: ST-elevation myocardial infarction within 12 hours of onset requires immediate reperfusion. Primary percutaneous coronary intervention is the preferred strategy where it can be delivered within 90 minutes of first medical contact, or 120 minutes from first medical contact if patient transfer is required. Fibrinolysis is used when PCI cannot be delivered within these windows, followed by routine angiography 3 to 24 hours later (pharmacoinvasive strategy). Dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin plus a P2Y12 inhibitor and anticoagulation are commenced before angiography.
key_points: Primary PCI preferred if door-to-balloon within 90 minutes onsite
Fibrinolysis with tenecteplase if PCI not available within 120 minutes from first contact
Pharmacoinvasive strategy: angiography 3 to 24 hours after successful fibrinolysis
Dual antiplatelet loading and anticoagulation before angiography
Cardiogenic shock or mechanical complications mandate immediate angiography regardless of timing
explanation.pearls: Failed fibrinolysis (under 50% ST resolution at 90 minutes) mandates rescue PCI as soon as possible, distinct from the routine pharmacoinvasive angiography window.

stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 42-year-old non-Aboriginal woman with three days of productive cough, fever and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. She has no significant past medical history and no recent hospital admission. She is not pregnant. She lives independently and her partner can supervise her at home. Her SMART-COP score is 0. She is up to date with COVID-19 and influenza vaccinations.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 °C, HR 96, BP 124/74, RR 20, SpO2 96% room air
data_table.Examination: Right lower zone crackles and bronchial breath sounds, no oxygen requirement, normal mental state, no signs of dehydration
data_table.Investigations: Chest x-ray: right lower lobe consolidation; rapid antigen testing for SARS-CoV-2 and influenza negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic?

options.text: Oral azithromycin 500 mg once daily for three days as monotherapy for atypical and typical coverage in the outpatient setting
options.rationale: Azithromycin monotherapy is inadequate empirical cover for typical Streptococcus pneumoniae because of high macrolide resistance rates in Australia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 500 mg four times daily for 7 days because it provides broad-spectrum cover for both Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae
options.rationale: Cefalexin has poor pneumococcal coverage compared with amoxicillin and is not the recommended first-line agent for community-acquired pneumonia in Australia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral moxifloxacin 400 mg daily for seven days as broad-spectrum coverage including atypical pathogens in the community setting given the productive cough
options.rationale: Respiratory fluoroquinolones are reserved for severe pneumonia, beta-lactam allergy or proven resistant organisms; routine outpatient use drives resistance and adverse effects. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily for five days with general practice review at 48 hours and safety netting for worsening breathlessness or systemic features
options.rationale: High-dose oral amoxicillin is the first-line agent for low-severity community-acquired pneumonia (SMART-COP 0 to 2) in Australia, with 5 day duration; doxycycline is the macrolide-equivalent alternative for atypical cover when needed. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for seven days because of broader beta-lactamase coverage given right lower lobe consolidation on imaging
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for moderate severity, aspiration risk or specific resistant pathogens; it is not first line for low-severity community-acquired pneumonia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: SMART-COP triages community-acquired pneumonia severity and disposition: scores of 0 to 2 are low risk and suitable for outpatient management. High-dose oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily for 5 days is first line in Australia. Doxycycline is added or substituted if atypical pathogens are suspected (Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila). Moxifloxacin is reserved for beta-lactam allergy or severe disease. SMART-COP 3 to 4 prompts hospital admission, often with intravenous ceftriaxone plus oral doxycycline or azithromycin; SMART-COP 5 or more prompts ICU consideration.
key_points: SMART-COP 0 to 2: outpatient oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily for 5 days
Doxycycline added if atypical pathogens suspected (Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila)
Moxifloxacin reserved for severe penicillin allergy or severe disease
SMART-COP 3 to 4 triggers admission for intravenous therapy
Review at 48 hours and escalate if no improvement
explanation.pearls: SMART-COP allocates 2 points each for systolic BP under 90, oxygen saturation under 90% (or under 94% if under 50 years), and pH under 7.35, while age threshold lowers SpO2 cut-off and other variables get 1 point each.

subtopic_detail: New atrial fibrillation rate versus rhythm control
stem: You are the general medicine registrar reviewing a 68-year-old non-Aboriginal man with palpitations and breathlessness for three weeks. He has hypertension and type 2 diabetes. He has had no chest pain or syncope. His exercise tolerance has fallen from 1 km to 200 m. His CHA2DS2-VA score is 4 and he has no bleeding risk factors. He has no history of heart failure or asthma.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132 irregular, BP 138/84, RR 18, SpO2 97% room air
data_table.Examination: Irregularly irregular pulse, chest clear, no peripheral oedema, no thyroid swelling, no carotid bruit
data_table.Investigations: ECG atrial fibrillation rate 130, TSH normal, echocardiogram pending, electrolytes normal, troponin normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management strategy?

options.text: Immediate DC cardioversion in the emergency department under conscious sedation and discharge on aspirin 100 mg daily without anticoagulation
options.rationale: Cardioversion without preceding 3 weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation or transoesophageal echocardiogram exclusion of left atrial thrombus risks embolic stroke; aspirin is not adequate for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation. (Source: NHFA AF Guidelines)

options.text: Commence oral amiodarone 200 mg three times daily for one week then 200 mg daily and start aspirin 100 mg with cardiology outpatient review in three months
options.rationale: Amiodarone is reserved for specialist-initiated rhythm control because of significant adverse effects, and aspirin is inadequate stroke prevention with CHA2DS2-VA 4. (Source: NHFA AF Guidelines)

options.text: Start digoxin 125 micrograms daily for rate control and aspirin 100 mg daily for stroke prevention with review in two weeks to assess heart rate and symptoms
options.rationale: Digoxin is second line for rate control because it controls only resting rate poorly during activity, and aspirin is inadequate anticoagulation for CHA2DS2-VA 4. (Source: NHFA AF Guidelines)

options.text: Commence apixaban 5 mg twice daily, metoprolol 25 mg twice daily titrated for rate control and arrange cardiology review for rhythm-control consideration
options.rationale: Apixaban is first-line anticoagulation in non-valvular atrial fibrillation with CHA2DS2-VA at least 2, and metoprolol is the standard rate-control agent; rhythm control via specialist review remains an option. (Source: NHFA AF Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for urgent electrophysiology study and pulmonary vein isolation ablation without commencing anticoagulation in the interim while awaiting the procedure
options.rationale: Catheter ablation is reserved for symptomatic rhythm control after failure of antiarrhythmic therapy and never replaces stroke-prevention anticoagulation. (Source: NHFA AF Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Initial management of new atrial fibrillation prioritises stroke risk assessment and rate control. Stroke risk by CHA2DS2-VA: a score of 2 or more in men or women warrants oral anticoagulation with a direct oral anticoagulant (apixaban, rivaroxaban or dabigatran) unless contraindicated, with warfarin reserved for moderate to severe mitral stenosis or mechanical valves. Rate control with a beta-blocker or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker is first line; rhythm control with cardioversion, flecainide, sotalol or amiodarone is considered when symptoms persist despite rate control, and catheter ablation is an option in symptomatic recurrent disease.
key_points: CHA2DS2-VA score of 2 or more triggers oral anticoagulation
DOACs first line for non-valvular AF; warfarin for mechanical valves and severe mitral stenosis
Aspirin has no role in atrial fibrillation stroke prevention
Metoprolol or atenolol first line for rate control
Non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers avoided in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
explanation.pearls: Apixaban dose is reduced to 2.5 mg twice daily if two of: age 80 years or older, body weight 60 kg or less, creatinine 133 micromol/L or higher.
